skill.name: firecrawl-interact
skill.description: Control and interact with a live browser session on any scraped page — click buttons, fill forms, navigate flows, and extract data using natural language prompts or code. Use when the user needs to interact with a webpage beyond simple scraping: logging into a site, submitting forms, clicking through pagination, handling infinite scroll, navigating multi-step checkout or wizard flows, or when a regular scrape failed because content is behind JavaScript interaction. Also useful for authenticated scraping via profiles. Triggers on "interact", "click", "fill out the form", "log in to", "sign in", "submit", "paginated", "next page", "infinite scroll", "interact with the page", "navigate to", "open a session", or "scrape failed".
skill.summary: Control and interact with a live browser session on any scraped page — click buttons, fill forms, navigate flows, and extract data using natural language prompts or code.
skill.author: firecrawl

security_audit.summary: Security evaluation confirms this skill is safe for publication. All 31 static findings are false positives. The static analyzer misidentified markdown CLI command documentation as Ruby backtick execution, example URLs as hardcoded network endpoints, and markdown relative links as path traversal vulnerabilities. The skill uses the firecrawl CLI tool to control a live browser session, which is the intended and documented functionality. No malicious intent detected.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentification: External Commands
high_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 19 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at lines 17, 20, 24, 39-50, 54, 56, 64, 68, 70, 74-76. These are markdown code blocks containing firecrawl CLI command examples like 'firecrawl scrape', 'firecrawl interact'. The scanner detected backtick syntax but incorrectly identified these as Ruby execution patterns.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown code blocks containing CLI commands like 'firecrawl scrape' are documented examples, not code execution. The backtick syntax is for markdown code formatting, not Ruby backtick operators.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentification: Hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 3 locations as 'Hardcoded URL' at lines 58, 62, 69. These are example URLs like 'https://app.example.com/login' used in documentation to demonstrate the --profile feature. Example URLs in documentation are not security vulnerabilities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Example URLs using app.example.com (a standard example domain) in documentation are expected and safe. These are not real network endpoints.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentification: Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 3 locations as 'Path traversal sequence' at lines 81, 82, 83. These are markdown relative links to related skill documentation files: '../firecrawl-scrape/SKILL.md', '../firecrawl-search/SKILL.md', '../firecrawl-agent/SKILL.md'. This is standard markdown linking, not path traversal vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown relative links to related skill documentation files are legitimate linking patterns, not filesystem path traversal vulnerabilities.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Misidentification: Browser Storage Access
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged line 54 as 'Browser storage access'. This line documents the --profile feature which persists browser state (cookies, localStorage). This is a documented product feature for authenticated scraping, not a vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Browser storage access for session persistence is a documented feature. The skill is a web scraping tool and this functionality is its intended purpose.

content.user_title: Interact with web pages using a live browser
content.value_statement: Static scraping fails when content is hidden behind JavaScript interactions. This skill lets you control a live browser to click buttons, fill forms, log into sites, and extract data from dynamic pages.
actual_capabilities: Click buttons and links on web pages using natural language prompts
Fill form fields with text and submit forms automatically
Navigate through multi-step flows like checkouts or wizards
Extract data from pages that require JavaScript interaction
Persist browser sessions for authenticated scraping with profiles
Stop browser sessions to free resources when done
limitations: Requires a prior scrape to obtain a session ID before interacting
Cannot be used for web searches; use the search command instead
Interaction timeout defaults to 30 seconds with a maximum of 300 seconds

use_cases.title: Automate login and authenticated scraping
use_cases.description: Log into password-protected sites using browser profiles. Store session state including cookies and localStorage. Return to authenticated pages without re-logging in each time.
use_cases.target_user: Developers scraping data from membership sites, portals, or authenticated APIs

use_cases.title: Handle JavaScript-rendered content
use_cases.description: When a standard scrape returns empty content, use interact mode to load the page in a real browser. Click load more buttons, expand accordions, or trigger infinite scroll to reveal hidden content.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers extracting content from SPAs and JavaScript-heavy sites

use_cases.title: Navigate multi-step web flows
use_cases.description: Complete complex web workflows like checkout processes, onboarding wizards, or multi-page forms. Each step is executed sequentially in the same browser session.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and automation specialists testing web application flows

prompt_templates.title: Click a button or link
prompt_templates.prompt: Click the login button on the page
prompt_templates.scenario: When a page has content hidden behind a button click

prompt_templates.title: Fill and submit a form
prompt_templates.prompt: Fill in the email field with my-email@example.com and click the submit button
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to fill out login, contact, or registration forms

prompt_templates.title: Extract data after interaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Click load more until all results are visible, then extract the product pricing table
prompt_templates.scenario: When content needs to be scrolled or expanded before extraction

prompt_templates.title: Navigate authenticated pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com/dashboard and extract the user statistics from the main panel
prompt_templates.scenario: When accessing pages behind authentication with a saved profile

output_examples.input: firecrawl interact --prompt "Click the contact button and extract the phone number"
output_examples.output: Successfully clicked the contact button. Extracted phone number: +1-555-0123

output_examples.input:
firecrawl scrape "https://app.example.com/login" --profile my-app
firecrawl interact --prompt "Fill in email with user@example.com and click login"
output_examples.output: Login successful. Profile saved. Subsequent scrapes will maintain authenticated session.

best_practices: Always run scrape first to establish a session ID before using interact commands
Use browser profiles for authenticated scraping to avoid repeated logins
Call firecrawl interact stop when finished to free system resources
anti_patterns: Do not use interact for web searches; use the search command instead
Avoid long interaction chains without checking intermediate results
Do not skip the scrape step; interact requires an active scraping session

faq.question: Why did the scrape return empty content?
faq.answer: The page likely uses JavaScript to render content. Use interact mode to load the page in a real browser and interact with dynamic elements.

faq.question: How do I log into a site that requires authentication?
faq.answer: Use the --profile flag with scrape to save browser state. Then use the same profile name for subsequent scrapes to stay authenticated.

faq.question: What happens if an interaction times out?
faq.answer: The default timeout is 30 seconds. You can increase it with --timeout up to 300 seconds for slow-loading content.

faq.question: Can I interact with multiple pages in parallel?
faq.answer: Yes. Scrape multiple pages first, then interact with each using --scrape-id to target specific sessions.

faq.question: How do I stop an active browser session?
faq.answer: Run firecrawl interact stop to free resources. Always stop sessions when done to avoid memory leaks.

faq.question: Why is interact failing even after a successful scrape?
faq.answer: The scrape session may have expired. Re-run the scrape to get a fresh session ID before using interact.
